title: Kansas City · Halved
subtitle: FBI UCR 1985–2014 + KCPD reports 2015–2026 · 240 neighborhoods · real locations

hook.line: Kansas City crime, from the 1991 peak to 2014
hook.sub: FBI UCR · then the map, month by month, up close

punchline.sub: −51% under the same FBI measure · KCPD's own broader count begins in 2015

copy.seamExplain: The ruler changes here — not the city. Through 2014 the gray bars are the FBI's classic 'index' crimes (murder, robbery, assault, burglary, theft). From 2015 the figures are KCPD's own reports — every offense type, deduplicated to one count per report — a broader net, so the level steps up. Compare the shape within one color, never across the dashed line.
copy.chapter2Kicker: CHAPTER 2 · THE LAST 5 YEARS · KCPD OPEN DATA
copy.chapter2Caption: Monthly reports, 2021–2026. Every dot is a real reported incident location, block-level, sampled.
copy.transitionDesc: The chart counted per year. From here the map counts per month across 240 neighborhoods — every dot a real reported location at block level.
copy.methodRecentSub: the last five years · month by month · real locations
copy.methodDotsSub: Every dot is a real reported incident's block, as geocoded by KCPD. 86.8% of reports map to one of 240 named neighborhoods — the rest (mostly missing coordinates) counted, disclosed, never invented.
copy.methodFootnote: FBI UCR (through 2014) and KCPD's deduplicated reports (2015+) are different measures — shown as two chapters, not one line.
copy.quizQuestion: Which Kansas City neighborhood reports the fewest crimes?
copy.sourceLine: Data: Kansas City, MO Police Department via data.kcmo.org · real block-level locations
copy.coverageText: 86.8% of records mapped to a neighborhood
copy.creditsSources: Sources: KCPD crime data 2015–2026 (12 yearly datasets; public domain where stated, the 2015–2017 assets carry no license field — attributed to KCPD) via data.kcmo.org & FBI UCR (1985–2014) via the FBI Crime Data Explorer (ORI MOKPD0000). KCPD publishes one row per involvement — every count here is deduplicated to one per report. Categories follow the NIBRS crimes-against convention (robbery counts against property). Neighborhoods: City of Kansas City official boundaries, joined by KCPD's published coordinates. Long-arc totals are citywide; the map counts the 86.8% with usable coordinates inside a named neighborhood — the rest counted, disclosed, never invented. FBI UCR index crimes and KCPD reports are different measures, shown as two chapters and never equated. Basemap: © OpenStreetMap contributors (ODbL).

historyNotes.text: 2014: 28,885 — down 51% from 1991; the last FBI year.
2023: 62,400 — the high point of KCPD's own broader count.

annotations.text: Every dot is a real reported incident location — block-level addresses geocoded by KCPD.
July 2023 — the busiest month for reported crime in the last five years: 5,087 mapped reports.
CBD Downtown — Kansas City's busiest neighborhood for reported crime.
Red Bridge North: down 62% since 2022 — the city's biggest neighborhood drop.
Reported crime on the map has held roughly flat: 46,038 (2022) → 45,763 (2025).
